subject: Physics
q: Two charged conducting spheres of radii $a$ and $b$ are connected to each other by a conducting wire. The ratio of charges of the two spheres respectively is:
question: Two charged conducting spheres of radii $a$ and $b$ are connected to each other by a conducting wire. The ratio of charges of the two spheres respectively is:
solution:
Potential at surface will be same
$\begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{Kq}_1}{\mathrm{a}}=\frac{\mathrm{Kq}_2}{\mathrm{~b}} \\ & \frac{\mathrm{q}_1}{\mathrm{q}_2}=\frac{\mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{b}}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q:
A charge Q is distributed over three concentric spherical shells of radii a, b, c a<b<c such that their surface charge densities are equal to one another.

The total potential at a point at distance r from their common centre, where r<a, would be:
question:
A charge Q is distributed over three concentric spherical shells of radii a, b, c a<b<c such that their surface charge densities are equal to one another.

The total potential at a point at distance r from their common centre, where r<a, would be:
solution:
Surface charge densities of all sphere is given as q1a2=q2b2=q3c2=constant, where q1, q2 and q3 is charge on sphere of radius a, b and c respectively.

q1+q2+q3=Q

q1=a2(Q)a2+b2+c2

q2=b2(Q)a2+b2+c2

q3=c2Qa2+b2+c2

Potential at a point where r<a is given as V=Kq1a+Kq2b+Kq3c, where K is a constant.

V=14πε0Qa+b+ca2+b2+c2

subject: Physics
q: Two point charges $-4 \mu c$ and $4 \mu c$, constituting an electric dipole, are placed at $(-9,0,0) \mathrm{cm}$ and $(9,0,0) \mathrm{cm}$ in a uniform electric field of strength $10^4 \mathrm{NC}^{-1}$. The work done on the dipole in rotating it from the equilibrium through $180^{\circ}$ is :
question: Two point charges $-4 \mu c$ and $4 \mu c$, constituting an electric dipole, are placed at $(-9,0,0) \mathrm{cm}$ and $(9,0,0) \mathrm{cm}$ in a uniform electric field of strength $10^4 \mathrm{NC}^{-1}$. The work done on the dipole in rotating it from the equilibrium through $180^{\circ}$ is :
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{U}=-\mathrm{PE} \cos \theta \\ & \mathrm{w}_{\mathrm{ext}}=\Delta \mathrm{U}=\mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{f}}-\mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{i}}=-\mathrm{PE} \cos 180^{\circ}+\mathrm{PE} \cos 0^{\circ} \\ & \mathrm{w}_{\mathrm{ext}}=2 \mathrm{PE} \\ & =2 \times\left(4 \times 10^{-6}\right)(18) \times 10^4 \\ & =144 \times 10^{-2} \\ & =14.4 \mathrm{~mJ}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: If the electric potential at any point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>y</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>z</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> in space is given by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> volt. The electric field at the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> will be :
question: If the electric potential at any point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>y</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>z</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> in space is given by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> volt. The electric field at the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> will be :
solution:
Electric potential V=3x2

Electric potential and electric field are related as E=-dVdx=-6x.

At the point 1, 0, 3 m, 

⇒E=-6×1=-6 V m-1

Thus, electric field is 6 V m-1 along negative x‐ axis.

subject: Physics
q: A simple pendulum of length L is placed between the plates of a parallel plate capacitor having electric field  E , as shown in figure. Its bob has mass m and charge  q . The time period of the pendulum is given by:
question: A simple pendulum of length L is placed between the plates of a parallel plate capacitor having electric field  E , as shown in figure. Its bob has mass m and charge  q . The time period of the pendulum is given by:
solution:
Two forces will be acting on the mass m, gravity force and force by the electric field.

Thus, the effective force =  qE2+mg2
From force equation, geff=g2+qEm2
Time period of the pendulum is T=2πLgeff
=2π Lg2+qEm2

subject: Physics
q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>27</mn></math> identical drops are charged at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>22</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> each. They combine to form a bigger drop. The potential of the bigger drop will be _____  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>.
question: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>27</mn></math> identical drops are charged at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>22</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> each. They combine to form a bigger drop. The potential of the bigger drop will be _____  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>.
solution:
Total volume of the drop will remain unchanged,

So,  27×43πr3=43πR3

⇒R=3r

Potential of the small drops, 22=kqr

For bigger drop, potential will be

V=k27q3r=9kqr=9×22=198 V

subject: Physics
q:
Two electrons each are fixed at a distance 2d. A third charge proton placed at the midpoint is displaced slightly by a distance xx≪d perpendicular to the line joining the two fixed charges. Proton will execute simple harmonic motion having angular frequency:
(m= mass of charged particle)
question:
Two electrons each are fixed at a distance 2d. A third charge proton placed at the midpoint is displaced slightly by a distance xx≪d perpendicular to the line joining the two fixed charges. Proton will execute simple harmonic motion having angular frequency:
(m= mass of charged particle)
solution:
From the given condition, we have

Fnet q=-2Fq/qcosθ

Fnet q=-2·14πε0·q2d2+x22·xd2+x2

=-q22πε0xd2+x23/2

For x<<d,

Fnet q=-q22πε0 d3x

∴  a=-q22πε0·md3x

Comparing with equation of SHM a=-ω2x

∴  ω=q22πε0md3

subject: Physics
q: The electric field at point $\mathrm{p}$ due to an electric dipole is $\mathrm{E}$. The electric field at point $\mathrm{R}$ on equitorial line will be $\frac{\mathrm{E}}{x}$. The value of $x$ :
question: The electric field at point $\mathrm{p}$ due to an electric dipole is $\mathrm{E}$. The electric field at point $\mathrm{R}$ on equitorial line will be $\frac{\mathrm{E}}{x}$. The value of $x$ :
solution: $\begin{aligned} & E_P=\frac{2 K P}{r^3}=E \\ & E_R=\frac{K P}{(2 r)^3}=\frac{E}{16} \\ & x=16\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: Five charges $+q,+5 q,-2 q,+3 q$ and $-4 q$ are situated as shown in the figure. The electric flux due to this configuration through the surface $S$ is :
question: Five charges $+q,+5 q,-2 q,+3 q$ and $-4 q$ are situated as shown in the figure. The electric flux due to this configuration through the surface $S$ is :
solution:
As per gauss theorem,
$\begin{aligned}& \phi=\frac{\mathrm{q}_{\text {in }}}{\epsilon_0}=\frac{\mathrm{q}+(-2 \mathrm{q})+5 \mathrm{q}}{\epsilon_0} \\
& \frac{4 \mathrm{q}}{\epsilon_0}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q:
A small bob of mass 100 mg and charge $+10 \mu \mathrm{C}$ is connected to an insulating string of length 1 m. It is brought near to an infinitely long nonconducting sheet of charge density ' $\sigma$ ' as shown in figure. If string subtends an angle of $45^{\circ}$ with the sheet at equilibrium the charge density of sheet will be :
(Given, $\varepsilon_0=8.85 \times 10^{-12} \frac{\mathrm{~F}}{\mathrm{~m}}$ and acceleration due to gravity, $g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$)
question:
A small bob of mass 100 mg and charge $+10 \mu \mathrm{C}$ is connected to an insulating string of length 1 m. It is brought near to an infinitely long nonconducting sheet of charge density ' $\sigma$ ' as shown in figure. If string subtends an angle of $45^{\circ}$ with the sheet at equilibrium the charge density of sheet will be :
(Given, $\varepsilon_0=8.85 \times 10^{-12} \frac{\mathrm{~F}}{\mathrm{~m}}$ and acceleration due to gravity, $g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$)
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{qE}=\mathrm{mg} \\ & \mathrm{q}\left[\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_0}\right]=\mathrm{mg} \\ & \sigma=\frac{2 \varepsilon_0 \mathrm{mg}}{\mathrm{q}} \\ & \sigma=\frac{2 \times 8.85 \times 10^{-12} \times 100 \times 10^{-6} \times 10}{10 \times 10^{-6}} \\ & \sigma=17.7 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{C} / \mathrm{m}^2 \\ & \sigma=1.77 \mathrm{nC} / \mathrm{m}^2\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: If two charges <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>q</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math>and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>q</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> are separated with distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi></math> and placed in a medium of dielectric constant <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>k</mi></math>. What will be the equivalent distance between charges in air for the same electrostatic force?
question: If two charges <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>q</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math>and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>q</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> are separated with distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi></math> and placed in a medium of dielectric constant <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>k</mi></math>. What will be the equivalent distance between charges in air for the same electrostatic force?
solution:
The force between the two charges when they are placed in dielectric is

F1=14πεokq1q2 d2

The force between the two charges when they are placed at separation d' is

F2=14πεoq1q2 d'2 

Since, the force between the two charges is same in both case

F1=F2

⇒14πεok     q1q2 d2= 14πεo q1q2 d'2

⇒d'=dk

subject: Physics
q:
As shown in the figure, a configuration of two equal point charges q0=+2μC is placed on an inclined plane. Mass of each point charge is 20 g. Assume that there is no friction between charge and plane. For the system of two point charges to be in equilibrium (at rest) the height h=x×10-3 m. The value of x is
 Take 14πε0=9×109 N m2 C-2, g=10 m s-2
question:
As shown in the figure, a configuration of two equal point charges q0=+2μC is placed on an inclined plane. Mass of each point charge is 20 g. Assume that there is no friction between charge and plane. For the system of two point charges to be in equilibrium (at rest) the height h=x×10-3 m. The value of x is
 Take 14πε0=9×109 N m2 C-2, g=10 m s-2
solution:
For the condition of equilibrium, the Coulomb force is equal to mgsinθ. Hence,

kq024h2=(mgsinθ)⇒kq024h2=20×10-3×10×12

⇒9×109×4×10−124h2=20×10-3×10×12⇒h=9100 m=0.3 m=300×10-3 m

h2=9100⇒h=310m=0.3m

=300×10−3m

subject: Physics
q: Two point charges $2 q$ and $q$ are placed at vertex $A$ and centre of face $C D E F$ of the cube as shown in figure. The electric flux passing through the cube is :
question: Two point charges $2 q$ and $q$ are placed at vertex $A$ and centre of face $C D E F$ of the cube as shown in figure. The electric flux passing through the cube is :
solution:
Using Gauss's law:
$\phi = \frac{Q_{in}}{\epsilon_0}$

Charge $2q$ is at vertex A of the cube. A vertex is shared by 8 cubes, so the flux contribution from $2q$ through this cube is $\frac{2q}{8\epsilon_0} = \frac{q}{4\epsilon_0}$.

Charge $q$ is at the centre of face CDEF. A face center is shared by 2 cubes, so the flux contribution from $q$ through this cube is $\frac{q}{2\epsilon_0}$.

Total flux:
$\phi = \frac{q/4 + q/2}{\epsilon_0} = \frac{3q}{4\epsilon_0}$

subject: Physics
q: For a uniformly charged ring of radius <math><mi>R</mi><mo>,</mo></math> the electric field on its axis has the largest magnitude at a distance <math><mi>h</mi></math> from its centre. Then value of <math><mi>h</mi></math> is:
question: For a uniformly charged ring of radius <math><mi>R</mi><mo>,</mo></math> the electric field on its axis has the largest magnitude at a distance <math><mi>h</mi></math> from its centre. Then value of <math><mi>h</mi></math> is:
solution:
Electric field at a distance h from centre on axis

E=14πε0QhR2+h232

To get maximum E, dEdh=0

0=14πε0QR2+h232-Qh32R2+h2122hR2+h23

⇒h=±R2

subject: Physics
q: A liquid drop having 6 excess electrons is kept stationary under a uniform electric field of $25.5$ $\mathrm{kVm}^{-1}$. The density of liquid is $1.26 \times 10^3 \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~m}^{-3}$. The radius of the drop is (neglect buoyancy).
question: A liquid drop having 6 excess electrons is kept stationary under a uniform electric field of $25.5$ $\mathrm{kVm}^{-1}$. The density of liquid is $1.26 \times 10^3 \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~m}^{-3}$. The radius of the drop is (neglect buoyancy).
solution:
$\mathrm{F}=\mathrm{qE}=m g\left(\mathrm{q}=6 \mathrm{e}=6 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}\right)$
$\operatorname{Density}(\mathrm{d})=\frac{\text { mass }}{\text { volume }}=\frac{\mathrm{m}}{\frac{4}{3} \pi \mathrm{r}^3}$
or $r^3=\frac{m}{\frac{4}{3} \pi d}$
Putting the value of $d$ and $m\left(=\frac{q E}{g}\right)$ and
solving we get $\mathrm{r}=7.8 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~m}$

subject: Physics
q: Two thin wires rings each having a radius $R$ are placed at a distance $d$ apart with their axes coinciding. The charges on the two rings are $+q$ and $-q$. The potential difference between the centres of the two rings is
question: Two thin wires rings each having a radius $R$ are placed at a distance $d$ apart with their axes coinciding. The charges on the two rings are $+q$ and $-q$. The potential difference between the centres of the two rings is
solution:
$v_1=\frac{k q}{R}-\frac{k q}{\sqrt{R^2+d^2}}$
$v_2=\frac{-k q}{R}+\frac{k q}{\sqrt{R^2+d^2}}$

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R

Assertion A: In electrostatics, a conductor does not store any net charge inside.

Reason R: Inside the capacitor (with no dielectric medium), the free charge carriers, if placed between the plates of capacitor, experience force and drift.

Choose the correct answer from the options given below
question:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R

Assertion A: In electrostatics, a conductor does not store any net charge inside.

Reason R: Inside the capacitor (with no dielectric medium), the free charge carriers, if placed between the plates of capacitor, experience force and drift.

Choose the correct answer from the options given below
options: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
solution:
In electrostatics, the electric field inside a conductor is zero. According to Gauss's law, $\oint \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{s} = \dfrac{q_{in}}{\epsilon_0}$. Since $\vec{E} = 0$ everywhere inside the conductor, the net charge enclosed $q_{in}$ must be zero. Thus, a conductor does not store any net charge inside its bulk, and any excess charge resides only on its surface. Assertion A is true.

Inside a capacitor with no dielectric medium, a uniform electric field exists between the plates. If free charge carriers are placed in this region, they will experience an electrostatic force $\vec{F} = q\vec{E}$ and will drift towards the respective oppositely charged plates. Reason R is true.

Although both statements are factually correct, the behavior of charge carriers in the electric field of a capacitor does not explain why the net charge inside a conductor is zero in electrostatics. Therefore, both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A.

Answer: Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A

subject: Physics
q:
Choose the incorrect statement:
(a) The electric lines of force entering into a Gaussian surface provide negative flux.
(b) A charge q is placed at the centre of a cube. The flux through all the faces will be the same.
(c) In a uniform electric field net flux through a closed Gaussian surface containing no net charge, is zero.
(d) When an electric field is parallel to a Gaussian surface, it provides a finite non-zero flux.

Choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
question:
Choose the incorrect statement:
(a) The electric lines of force entering into a Gaussian surface provide negative flux.
(b) A charge q is placed at the centre of a cube. The flux through all the faces will be the same.
(c) In a uniform electric field net flux through a closed Gaussian surface containing no net charge, is zero.
(d) When an electric field is parallel to a Gaussian surface, it provides a finite non-zero flux.

Choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
solution:
We know that electric flux is a scalar quantity and this is the scalar product of the magnitude of electric field and surface area. 

Recall, the formula given by Gauss theorem, ϕ=E→·A→=EAcosθ, here θ=90°, it means, ϕ=0.

subject: Physics
q:
Statement-I : A point charge is brought in an electric field. The value of electric field at a point near to the charge may increase if the charge is positive.

Statement-II : An electric dipole is placed in a uniform electric field. The net electric force on the dipole will not be zero.
question:
Statement-I : A point charge is brought in an electric field. The value of electric field at a point near to the charge may increase if the charge is positive.

Statement-II : An electric dipole is placed in a uniform electric field. The net electric force on the dipole will not be zero.
options: Both statement-I and statement-II are true.
Both statement-I and statement-II are false.
Statement-I is true but statement-II is false.
Statement-I is false but statement-II is true.
solution:
If a positive point charge is brought in an electric field. The value of electric field at a point near to the charge may increase or decreases. Hence, statement I is true.

An electric dipole experiences zero net force in a uniform electric field. Hence, statement II is false.

question: A cube is placed inside an electric field, E→=150y2j^ The side of the cube is 0.5 m and is placed in the field as shown in the given figure. The charge inside the cube is:
solution:
By gauss law ∮E→·ds→=qin ε0

ε0(150)(0.5)2×(0.5)2=qin

qin=8.85×10-12×15016=8.3×10-11C

Assertion (A) : The outer body of an air craft is made of metal which protects persons sitting inside from lightning-strikes.
Reason (R) : The electric field inside the cavity enclosed by a conductor is zero.

question:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other is labelled as Reason (R).
Assertion (A) : The outer body of an air craft is made of metal which protects persons sitting inside from lightning-strikes.
Reason (R) : The electric field inside the cavity enclosed by a conductor is zero.

$(\mathrm{A})$ is correct but $(\mathrm{R})$ is not correct
Both $(\mathrm{A})$ and $(\mathrm{R})$ are correct but $(\mathrm{R})$ is not correct explanation of (A)
(A) is not correct but $(\mathrm{R})$ is correct
solution: Electric field of outside charge is zero inside conductor

q: An electric charge <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> is placed at origin <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn><mo>)</mo></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Y</mi></math> co-ordinate system. Two points <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math> are situated at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt><mo>)</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><msqrt><mn>6</mn></msqrt><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn><mo>)</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> respectively. The potential difference between the points <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math> will be :
question: An electric charge <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> is placed at origin <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn><mo>)</mo></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Y</mi></math> co-ordinate system. Two points <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math> are situated at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt><mo>)</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><msqrt><mn>6</mn></msqrt><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn><mo>)</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> respectively. The potential difference between the points <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math> will be :
solution:
The formula to calculate the potential difference between P and Q is given by

∆V=kq1r1-1r2   ...1

Now,

r1=(3)2+(3)2 m=6 m

And,

r2=(6)2+0 m=6 m

As r1=r2=6 m and the value of the charge remains constant, the potential difference between the points is zero.

subject: Physics
q: A point charge $q=1 \mu \mathrm{C}$ is located at a distance 2 cm from one end of a thin insulating wire of length 10 cm having a charge $Q=24 \mu \mathrm{C}$, distributed uniformly along its length, as shown in figure. Force between $q$ and wire is $\_\_\_\_$ N. (Use : $\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_{\mathrm{o}}}=9 \times 10^{9} \mathrm{~N}. \mathrm{m}^{2} / \mathrm{C}^{2}$)
question: A point charge $q=1 \mu \mathrm{C}$ is located at a distance 2 cm from one end of a thin insulating wire of length 10 cm having a charge $Q=24 \mu \mathrm{C}$, distributed uniformly along its length, as shown in figure. Force between $q$ and wire is $\_\_\_\_$ N. (Use : $\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_{\mathrm{o}}}=9 \times 10^{9} \mathrm{~N}. \mathrm{m}^{2} / \mathrm{C}^{2}$)
solution:
Consider a small element of length $dx$ on the wire at a distance $x$ from the point charge $q$.
The linear charge density of the wire is $\lambda = \frac{Q}{L} = \frac{24 \times 10^{-6}}{0.1} = 240 \times 10^{-6}$ C/m.
The charge on the small element is $dQ = \lambda dx$.
The electrostatic force between the point charge $q$ and the element $dQ$ is $dF = \frac{k q dQ}{x^2} = \frac{k q \lambda dx}{x^2}$.
The total force $F$ is obtained by integrating $dF$ from $x = a$ to $x = a + L$, where $a = 2$ cm and $L = 10$ cm.
$F = \int_{0.02}^{0.12} \frac{k q \lambda}{x^2} dx = k q \lambda \left[ -\frac{1}{x} \right]_{0.02}^{0.12}$
$F = k q \lambda \left( \frac{1}{0.02} - \frac{1}{0.12} \right) = k q \frac{Q}{L} \left( \frac{0.12 - 0.02}{0.02 \times 0.12} \right)$
$F = \frac{k q Q}{L} \frac{L}{a(a+L)} = \frac{k q Q}{a(a+L)}$
Substituting the given values: $k = 9 \times 10^9$ N.m$^2$/C$^2$, $q = 1 \times 10^{-6}$ C, $Q = 24 \times 10^{-6}$ C, $a = 0.02$ m, and $a+L = 0.12$ m.
$F = \frac{9 \times 10^9 \times 1 \times 10^{-6} \times 24 \times 10^{-6}}{0.02 \times 0.12}$
$F = \frac{9 \times 24 \times 10^{-3}}{0.0024} = \frac{216 \times 10^{-3}}{2.4 \times 10^{-3}}$
$F = \frac{216}{2.4} = 90$ N.

subject: Physics
q: Which of the following correctly represents the variation of electric potential <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>V</mi><mo>)</mo></math> of a charged spherical conductor of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>R</mi><mo>)</mo></math> with radial distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>r</mi><mo>)</mo></math> from the centre?
question: Which of the following correctly represents the variation of electric potential <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>V</mi><mo>)</mo></math> of a charged spherical conductor of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>R</mi><mo>)</mo></math> with radial distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>r</mi><mo>)</mo></math> from the centre?
solution:
As electric field inside the charged conducting sphere is zero, therefore potential will be constant for the inside part.

For outside part, electric potential decreases as V=kqr and the conducting sphere behaves like a point charge concentrated at the centre.

subject: Physics
q: Two charges of each magnitude 0.01 C and separated by a distance of 0.4 mm constitute an electric dipole. If the dipole is placed in an uniform electric field E→ of 10 dyne·C-1 making 30∘ angle with E→, the magnitude of torque acting on dipole is:
question: Two charges of each magnitude 0.01 C and separated by a distance of 0.4 mm constitute an electric dipole. If the dipole is placed in an uniform electric field E→ of 10 dyne·C-1 making 30∘ angle with E→, the magnitude of torque acting on dipole is:
solution:
The formula to calculate the dipole moment is given by

p⇀=qd⇀   ...1

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (1) to calculate the dipole moment.

p⇀=0.01 C×0.4 mm×1 m103 mm= 4.0×10-6 C·m

The formula to calculate the torque on the dipole in the electric field is given by

|τ→|=|p→×E→|   ...2

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (2) to calculate the required torque.

τ⇀= 4.0×10-6 C·m×10 dyne·C-1× 1 N105 dyne×sin30°= 2×10-10 N m

subject: Physics
q:
Three identical charged balls each of charge 2C are suspended from a common point P by silk threads of 2 m each (as shown in figure). They form an equilateral triangle of side 1 m.

The ratio of net force on a charged ball to the force between any two charged balls will be
question:
Three identical charged balls each of charge 2C are suspended from a common point P by silk threads of 2 m each (as shown in figure). They form an equilateral triangle of side 1 m.

The ratio of net force on a charged ball to the force between any two charged balls will be
solution:
We have force between any two charge F1=F2=q24πε0l2=F

Resultant electrostatics force FR=F12+F22+2F1F2cos60°

=F2+F2+2F2×12=3F2

So, the ratio of net force on a charged ball to the force between any charged balls is 

  ⇒FRF=3

subject: Physics
q: Two charges of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mi>Q</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>Q</mi></math> are situated at the points <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mn>3</mn><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn><mo>)</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn><mo>)</mo></math> respectively. The electric flux through a sphere of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mi>a</mi></math> having centre at origin is:
question: Two charges of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mi>Q</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>Q</mi></math> are situated at the points <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mn>3</mn><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn><mo>)</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn><mo>)</mo></math> respectively. The electric flux through a sphere of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mi>a</mi></math> having centre at origin is:
solution:
As can be seen from the figure above, only the 5Q charge is inside the spherical region.

The formula to calculate the flux through the surface is given by

ϕ=qencε0   ...1

From equation (1), the flux through the given surface can be calculated as follows:

ϕ=5Qε0

subject: Physics
q: Three charges $+2 q,+3 q$ and $-4 q$ are situated at $(0,-3 a),(2 a, 0)$ and $(-2 a, 0)$ respectively in the $x y$ plane. The resultant dipole moment about origin is $\_\_\_\_$.
question: Three charges $+2 q,+3 q$ and $-4 q$ are situated at $(0,-3 a),(2 a, 0)$ and $(-2 a, 0)$ respectively in the $x y$ plane. The resultant dipole moment about origin is $\_\_\_\_$.
solution:
The dipole moment is given by:
$\vec{p} = q_1\vec{r}_1 + q_2\vec{r}_2 + q_3\vec{r}_3$
$\vec{p} = (2q)(-3a)\hat{j} + (3q)(2a)\hat{i} + (-4q)(-2a)\hat{i}$
$\vec{p} = -6qa\hat{j} + 6qa\hat{i} + 8qa\hat{i}$
$\vec{p} = 14qa\hat{i} - 6qa\hat{j}$
$\vec{p} = 2qa(7\hat{i} - 3\hat{j})$

subject: Physics
q: A solid conducting sphere, having a charge <math><mi mathvariant="normal">Q</mi><mo>,</mo></math> is surrounded by an uncharged conducting hollow spherical shell. Let the potential difference between the surface of the solid sphere and that of the outer surface of the hollow shell be <math><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If the shell is now given a charge of <math><mo>–</mo><mn>4</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">Q</mi><mo>,</mo></math> the new potential difference between the same two surfaces is:
question: A solid conducting sphere, having a charge <math><mi mathvariant="normal">Q</mi><mo>,</mo></math> is surrounded by an uncharged conducting hollow spherical shell. Let the potential difference between the surface of the solid sphere and that of the outer surface of the hollow shell be <math><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If the shell is now given a charge of <math><mo>–</mo><mn>4</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">Q</mi><mo>,</mo></math> the new potential difference between the same two surfaces is:
solution:
First condition:

Both conducting spheres are shown.
Vin-Vout=KQr1-KQr2
⇒V=KQ1r1-1r2
Second condition:

Shell is now given charge -4Q .
Vin-Vout=KQr1-4KQr2-KQr2-4KQr2
=KQr1-KQr2
=KQ1r1-1r2=V
